request.question: Imiliya is a town located in what pilgrimage site?

title: Kushinagar
Pushkar
Dobbaspet
Gajpanth
Kundalpur
Lumbini
Imiliya
Chakia, Bihar
Giruwari
Grishneshwar
sentences: Kushinagar, Kusinagar or Kusinara is a pilgrimage town and a Notified Area Council in the Kushinagar district of the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh located around NH-28, and is 52 km east of Gorakhpur city.
It is an important Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained Parinirvana after his death.
Outside India, it is an International Buddhist Pilgrimage Centre.
The followers of Buddhism, especially from Asian countries, wish to visit this place at least once in their lifetime.
Pushkar (Hindi: पुष्कर ) is a town in the Ajmer district in the Indian state of Rajasthan.
It is situated 14 km northwest of Ajmer at an average elevation of 510 m and is one of the five sacred dhams (pilgrimage site) for devout Hindus.

According to Hindu theology, the pond at the Katas Raj Temples near Choa Saidan Shah in Chakwal District of Pakistan has a theological association with Shiva; it was formed by the tears of Lord Shiva which he is believed to have shed after the death of his wife, Sati.

The story goes that when Sati died, Shiva cried so much and for so long, that his tears created two holy ponds – one at Pushkara in Ajmer in India and the other at Ketaksha, which literally means raining eyes, in Sanskrit.
It is from this name that the word Katas is derived.
It is often called "Tirth Raj" – the king of pilgrimage sites – and has in recent years become a popular destination for foreign tourists.
Dobbspet is a tiny town located in Nelamangala Taluk, Bangalore Rural District, Karnataka, India.

formerly called as Somapura re named as Dobbspet in memory of Major General Richard Stewart Dobbs first collector of Tumkur District, who is an eminent statesman who devoted his major part of life for the service of the People by the establishment of order and systematic administration in Tumkur district.
Dobbspet is located 50 km away from Bangalore along National Highway 4.
Nearest city is Tumkur.
Hindu pilgrimage site of Shivagange lies near the town.
Gajpanth (also spelled "Gajpantha") is a Jain pilgrimage site ("Sidh-ak-shetra") located near the Jain temple at Nashik.
It is located in Masrul village, in the Nashik district of the Indian state of Maharashtra.
It is 16 km from the Nashik Road Railway station and 5 km from Nashik City, and is situated on the steep slopes of a 400 ft hill.
The pilgrimage site is accessible via a staircase built in black stone, which leads directly to the temple.
The hill has 450 steps, three caves (known as "' chamar leni' "), and several temples belonging to the Digambara sect of the Jains.
There is also a sculpture depicting samavasarana (divine preaching hall of tirthankara) on the hills of Gajpanth.
Kundalpur is a town located in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, 35 km from the city of Damoh.
It is a historical pilgrimage site for Jains in India.
Lumbinī (Nepali and Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी    , "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.
It is the place where, according to Buddhist tradition, Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.
Gautama, who achieved Enlightenment some time around 528 BCE, became the Buddha and founded Buddhism.
Lumbini is one of many magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha.
Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.
Chakia is one of the important and commercial towns and a Nagar Panchayat in Purvi Champaran district in the state of Bihar, India.
Chakia is sub divisional headquarter town and also block headquarter.
During the British rule, Champaran Sugar Mill was founded in 1905.
sugar cane mill is now defunct since 1995.
Chakia is one of the many gateways to nearby villages.
Cloths and food grain traded between near by villages and Chakia.
Chakia now is a rapidly growing town.

Chakia has many old and famous places of worship like Shree Rani Sati Mandir, Baudhi Devi Mandir, Balkhana Devi Mandir, Durga Mandir, Maruti Mandir (Bife Bazaar), Hanumaan Mandir (Bara Bazaar), Noori Masjid (Near Railway Station), Jama Masjid and many more.
The town down area of chakia are Keshria Road, Sahebganj Road, Station Road, Motihari Road, Hospital Road and Madhuban Road.
There areas are densely populated with small shops as well as branded or non-branded shop selling various types of goods and services.
The associate Post Office is called Bara Chakia.
This town is served by a broad gauge train and connected by NH-28.
The railway station is called Chakia.
It is also the closest railway station to Kesaria Stupa, a Buddhist pilgrimage site.
Giruwari sometimes known as Girwari Jhyalbas ( गिरुवारी ) is one of the village located in north part of Nawalparasi district of Lumnini Zone in the western development region of Nepal.
Lumbinī (Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी, "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi district of Nepal.
[1] It is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama, who is the Buddha.
Gautama founded the Buddhist tradition.
Buddha lived between roughly 563 and 483 BC.
Lumbini is one of four magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha, the others being at Kushinagar, Bodh Gaya and Sarnath.
Grishneshwar temple, sometimes referred to as the Ghrneshwar Jyotirlinga or Dhushmeshwar temple, is one of the 12 Jyotirlinga shrines mentioned in the Shiva Purana.
The word "Ghrneshwar" means "lord of compassion".
The temple is an important pilgrimage site in Shaivism tradition of Hinduism, which considers it as the last or twelfth Jyotirlinga (linga of light).
This pilgrimage site is located in Ellora (also called Verul), less than a kilometer from Ellora Caves – a UNESCO World Heritage site.
It is about 30 km north-west of the city of Aurangabad, and about 300 km east-northeast from Mumbai.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Imiliya is a town located in what pilgrimage site?
hop1_docs: Imiliya | Imiliya इमिलिया is a small town located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal.
Madhu, Sri Lanka | Madhu is town in Mannar District, Sri Lanka.  The Shrine of Our Lady of Madhu, a major site of Roman Catholic pilgrimage, is located in this town.

Jison-in | Jison-in (慈尊院 ) is a Buddhist temple in the town of Kudoyama that marks the entrance to the pilgrimage route of Koyasan.  It is part of the "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range" UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Kushinagar | Kushinagar, Kusinagar or Kusinara is a pilgrimage town and a Notified Area Council in the Kushinagar district of the Indian state of Uttar Pradesh located around NH-28, and is 52 km east of Gorakhpur city.  It is an important Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained Parinirvana after his death.  Outside India, it is an International Buddhist Pilgrimage Centre.  The followers of Buddhism, especially from Asian countries, wish to visit this place at least once in their lifetime.
Kundalpur | Kundalpur is a town located in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, 35 km from the city of Damoh.  It is a historical pilgrimage site for Jains in India.

Yoshino Mountain | Yoshino Mountain (吉野山 , Yoshino-yama ) is a mountain located in the town of Yoshino in Yoshino District, Nara Prefecture, Japan.  In 2004, It was designated as part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site under the name "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range".

Reward, Saskatchewan | 12 km south of Reward on Grid 675 is the site of the Shrine of the Holy Rosary. .  The site has been the location of an annual pilgrimage since 1932. About 3,000 people participated in the first pilgrimage.
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [1] quoted: "located in the Kapilvastu District, Lumbini, Nepal" — Imiliya is in Lumbini (Kapilvastu District).  
  - [1] paraphrase: Imiliya is a "small town" (Imiliya इमिलिया).  

- Bridge entity / missing or ambiguous facts:
  - Bridge entity: Lumbini (the place Imiliya is located).  
  - Missing confirmation: none of the supplied passages explicitly state that Lumbini is a "pilgrimage site" (passages do not include a Lumbini article or state its pilgrimage status).  
  - Ambiguity: "Imiliya" has a single entry in the passages (not ambiguous).

- Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve the missing confirmation:
  1. Fetch the "Lumbini" page (Wikipedia or authoritative source). Extract from the lead/intro or sections titled "Significance", "History", or "Religion" a statement that Lumbini is a Buddhist pilgrimage site (e.g., "birthplace of the Buddha", "pilgrimage site", or "UNESCO World Heritage Site").  
  2. (If needed) Fetch the full "Imiliya" page/article to confirm the locality text and any mention linking it to pilgrimage tourism or sites within Lumbini. Extract the lead or "Location" paragraph.  
  3. (Optional) Fetch "Kapilvastu District" page; check "Tourism" or "Geography" sections for references tying Imiliya or local towns to the Lumbini pilgrimage complex.

- Final answer withheld here because the passages do not explicitly label Lumbini as a pilgrimage site; fetch target (1) to confirm.
response.hop2_query: Lumbini page lead confirm pilgrimage site
hop2_docs: Buddhist pilgrimage sites in Nepal | Lumbini is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.  There are number of historical Buddhist pilgrimage sites in Nepal.

Lumbini | Lumbinī (Nepali and Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी    , "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi District of Nepal.  It is the place where, according to Buddhist tradition, Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama in 563 BCE.  Gautama, who achieved Enlightenment some time around 528 BCE, became the Buddha and founded Buddhism.  Lumbini is one of many magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha.

Rishikesh Complex of Ruru Kshetra | The Rishikesh Complex of Ruru Kshetra is located in southwestern Nepal in the Palpa District, Lumbini Zone.  It is an important Hindu pilgrimage as well as cremation site referred to in the Hindu epics.

Giruwari | Giruwari sometimes known as Girwari Jhyalbas ( गिरुवारी ) is one of the village located in north part of Nawalparasi district of Lumnini Zone in the western development region of Nepal.  Lumbinī (Sanskrit: लुम्बिनी, "the lovely") is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in the Rupandehi district of Nepal. [1] It is the place where Queen Mayadevi gave birth to Siddhartha Gautama, who is the Buddha.  Gautama founded the Buddhist tradition.  Buddha lived between roughly 563 and 483 BC.  Lumbini is one of four magnets for pilgrimage that sprang up in places pivotal to the life of the Buddha, the others being at Kushinagar, Bodh Gaya and Sarnath.

Lumbini Buddhist University | Lumbini Buddhist University is a tertiary educational institution currently under construction in Lumbini, Nepal, the birthplace of the Buddha.  The idea for the university was conceived at the First World Buddhist Summit held in Lumbini in 1998, and it was officially formed in November 2004.  The Lumbini Buddhist University Act promulgated on November 10, 2006 confirmed its legal status as well as setting out that the university would receive financial assistance from the government of Nepal.

Kumano Hongū Taisha | Kumano Hongū Taisha (熊野本宮大社 ) is a Shinto shrine located in Tanabe, Wakayama Prefecture, deep in the rugged mountains of the Kii Peninsula of Japan.  It is included as part of the Kumano Sanzan in the UNESCO World Heritage site "Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range".  The main deity enshrined is Kumano Gongen (熊野権現).  All of the ancient Kumano Kodō Pilgrimage routes lead to this Grand Shrine.

Junrei | Junrei (巡礼) is the word most commonly used for either of two major types of pilgrimages in Japan, in accordance with Buddhism or Shinto.  These pilgrimages can be made as a visit to a group of temples, shrines, or other holy sites, in a particular order, often in a circuit of 33 or 88 sites.  Other pilgrimages may center on a pilgrimage to a single site.  One of the most popular pilgrimages for Buddhists in Japan is visiting the 88 temples on Shikoku.
response.summary_2: Imiliya is located in Lumbini. Lumbini is a Buddhist pilgrimage site in Nepal, traditionally regarded as the birthplace of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha).
